Description: Fragment of painted limestone relief from the tomb of Sety I from a wall corner. Part of a goddess is preserved, depicted with yellow skin and a red dress, while on the other face, a portion of the king's titulary in two cartouches. Later hieratic graffito in ink near the arm of the goddess referring to Maat.;
Inscription: Inscription type: inscription Inscription script: hieroglyphic; Inscription type: inscription Inscription script: Hieratic Inscription translation: The one who protects Maat Inscription note: This is a later ink graffito left probably by a visitor to the tomb, possibly as identification of the goddess.;
Curators Comments:
This fragment may come from the right-hand door jamb at the entrance to Room I. It shows a similarity to a fragment depicting Maat now in the collection of the Museo Archeologico Nazionale di Firenze (Inv. no. 2469), which has been attributed to the opposite jamb.
Recent work on the reconstruction of the tomb including 3D scanning and survey of relief in museum collections has been undertaken by Factum Arte:
https://www.factumfoundation.org/pag/1502/the-tomb-of-seti-recording-and-facsimile; This fragment would have been part of a larger scene showing the pharaoh before the gods of the afterlife. 
The royal tomb of Sety I, from which this relief fragment derives, is the deepest and longest in the Valley of the Kings. It is richly decorated with painted reliefs as well as extensive texts: the first tomb to be decorated with a complete program of religious texts, including the Litany of Ra, Book of the Dead, Amduat, Book of Gates, and Opening of the Mouth ritual.

Description: Limestone false-door of Kaihap.;
Dimensions: 210.00 centimetres;  998.00 kilograms estimated ;  144.00 centimetres;  18.00 centimetres max depth ;
Inscription: Inscription type: inscription Inscription script: hieroglyphic Inscription translation: Titles/epithets include : King's Confidant; Chosen Inspector Of Those Who Are In Attendance;
Curators Comments:
Published: 
Martin, Hetepka pl. 21 (14), pp 20-1.
A.M. Donadoni Roveri and F. Tiradritti [eds.], Kemet: Alle Sorgenti del Tempo, Ravenna 1998, p.295 [300].
P. Pasini [ed.], Kemet: Guide all mostra, Ravenna 1998, p.28.
Art and Afterlife in Ancient Egypt, Japan 1999-2000, [18];
N. Strudwick, Masterpieces of Ancient Egypt, London 2006, pp. 64-5.; PM III (2): 448; Strudwick N 2006
This rather elaborate false door was made in two pieces, presumably for ease of manufacture, transport, and installation. It is all that survives of the chapel of Kaihap at Saqqara. The stela is unusual for its date because of the sheer number of people depicted on it, in addition to the inscriptions. The latter are conventional, consisting of a two-line funerary formula at the top, and names and titles elsewhere.
The central aspect of any false door is the central panel of the upper part of the false door. This one shows Kaihap and his wife Meretminu seated at a table, on which are stylized slices or thin loaves of bread; above these is a list of sacred substances. On either side of the couple is a row of five male and five female figures; their relationship to the couple is not specified, though they might be their children. The lower part of the door presents two large standing depictions of the couple, with their names and titles above and in front of them. Behind each depiction are four sub-registers of individuals, male on the right and male and female on the left; these are their grandchildren, six boys and three girls in all. Below the standing figures are ten men carrying offerings, all of whom are soul priests, responsible for the rituals to be performed in the tomb. A considerable amount of colour is preserved on the figures, with red used for males and yellow for females.
It is unusual for quite so many figures to appear on a door, and it seems probable that the entire decoration of the tomb was concentrated on this stela, a compression of the more standard compositions which might put children and priests on adjacent walls. Kaihap was a relatively minor official who would have been close to the bottom of the tomb-owning ladder, and he was probably delighted to have been able to commission a monument of this quality. His two titles on the door are 'royal acquaintance', denoting relatively lowly rank among those important enough to own a decorated tomb at this date, and the unusual 'inspector of those chosen to be in attendance'. Whom Kaihap attended is not clear, but it might have even been the king.
The tomb in which this false door was found was located roughly 100 metres southeast of the South Ibis catacombs, burial places for votive sacred birds in the Late Period and just one group of a number of animal cemeteries in this area, excavated in the 1960s and 1970s by the Egypt Exploration Society. The area had previously served as part of the Old Kingdom North Saqqara necropolis, containing tombs from the Third to the Sixth Dynasties.
The false door was given to Britain in acknowledgement of the country's contribution to the Nubian Rescue Campaign of the 1960s.
Bib references: Strudwick 2006 / Masterpieces of Ancient Egypt
Location: On display (Room 4 - Egyptian sculpture, Bay 24)

Description: Part of a limestone tomb-relief: parts of three registers carved in good, low relief are preserved. In the bottom register, on the right, is a scene of fishing with a drag-net from a boat; on the left are parts of a harvesting scene - three men are shown harvesting grain and two donkeys are being led away, their backs loaded with great sacks of grain. The centre register contains a scene in which young girls dance and boys play various games; one of the boys is shown wearing an unusual mask. The top register, of which only the lower half is preserved, originally contained a scene of boat-building; on the left, part of one boat is visible; the remainder of the register shows a number of workmen engaged in preparing timber for further construction. The block is in two pieces, the division running down the middle; some damage has occurred on each side of this cut. Some colour still remains: considerable traces of red on the bodies of the men, traces of yellow on the grain being harvested, a few spots of blue on the water below the boat in the bottom register.;
Inscription: Inscription type: inscription Inscription script: hieroglyphic Inscription note: In low relief.;
Bib references: James 1961 / Hieroglyphic texts from Egyptian stelae, etc. Part 1; Russmann 2001 / Eternal Egypt: Masterworks of Ancient Art from the British Museum

Description: Limestone tomb-relief of Nyibunesu: the slab bears a figure in strong, well-moulded low relief of Nyibunesu, shown wearing full official dress including a leopard-skin kilt. In front is a line of text with his name and titles. There are remains of black paint on the wig and on the armlet of the pendant arm and of red paint on the band across the chest.;
Inscription: Inscription type: inscription Inscription script: hieroglyphic Inscription transliteration: Name of deceased : Ny-ỉbw-nsw (with titles) Inscription translation: Name of deceased : Nyibunesu (with titles) Inscription note: In relief.;
Curators Comments:
This slab was found by Petrie in the tomb of Nyibunesu, at Dendera; it was placed in the innermost recess of the false door, above the tomb relief (1898,0817.11); there were no other texts or representations, cf. W. M. F. Petrie, 'Dendereh' (London, 1900),  pl. 2. The date of the tomb is by no means certain; it could be earlier than the Fourth Dynasty, but would then be an almost isolated example of very early tomb-sculpture from the necropolis of  Dendera.

B. Porter & R. Moss, 'Topographical Bibliography of Ancient Egyptian Hieroglyphic Texts, Reliefs and Paintings' V (Oxford: Clarendon Press),  p. 110.

Description: Limestone relief from tomb of Khnumhotep: this panel, which is carved in good, shallow, sunk relief, shows Khnumhotep seated before an offering-table loaded with conventionalized sliced loaves of bread; he is represented wearing official dress with leopard-skin. To the right and below the offering-table is an enumeration of funerary offerings. Above are the titles and name of Khnumhotep. Near the top is a single red line. Much colour still remains: the flesh of the principal figure is coloured red, the wig black, the leopard-skin yellow; the chair is yellow, the sliced loaves alternately yellow and black. Most of the signs are without colour but a few show traces of having been blue completely; one has a green top and dark blue bottom; the wig of the head symbol is black.;
Inscription: Inscription type: inscription Inscription script: hieroglyphic Inscription note: In painted relief.;

Description: A long-handled cutting tool of uncertain use: the cutting edges are on either side of the pointed tip, and only extend for about 5.0cm from the point up the length of the tool. Above the sharpened edges, the tool narrows, and then tapers gradually to a slightly convex butt. The edges of the metal all along the stem are rounded off smoothly, probably indicating that this part served as a handle, especially as the tool seems never to have been equipped with a separate handle of wood. The cutting edges have been ground sharp from both sides.;

Description: Greek style pottery trefoil-mouth oinochoe jug with ribbed neck; made in Egypt. Wheel-made and undecorated with repair holes. Ribbed base of neck, ovoid bottom-heavy body and broad ring foot and bifid handles. White and green residue on the inside, black residue on the outside. Red-brown Nile silt with a grey core, abundant gold mica and white inclusions.;
Inscription: Inscription type: inscription Inscription language: Greek Inscription quoted: vac δεκα vac Inscription note: Schlotzhauer suggests deka(te), 'tithe'.;
Curators Comments:
The piece has a close parallel from Naukratis (British Museum 1910,0222.232.b; Petrie 1886, pl. 34, no. 637) with the same graffito, probably an abbreviation of deka(te), 'tithe', i.e. a votive dedication. An alternative interpretation would be use as a measure; the jug  has a capacity of about 0.375 litres.
A further very similar piece with a Carian inscription is known from in Egypt (Berlin, Ägyptisches Museum und Papyrussammlung, Inv. 7206; Schlotzhauer 2012, 168 no. Nau 139, pl. 30c-e); with precise findspot information lacking it might have come from Naukratis or a site with a strong Carian presence such as Memphis/Saqqara.  For the latter two piecse, NA analysis by Hans Mommsen, Bonn, has confirmed production in the Nile Delta (samples Nauk 14 and 19: chemical group QanN), see Schlotzhauer and Villing 2006,55 table 1, 62-3, figs 35-6. The group of jugs was probably manufactured by Greek potters at Naukratis.
Bib references: Petrie 1886 / Naukratis. Part I., 1884-85; Schlotzhauer 2012 / Untersuchungen zur archaischen griechischen Keramik aus Naukratis; Villing et al. 2013-2025 / Naukratis: Greeks in Egypt; Leclère and Spencer 2014 / Tell Dafana Reconsidered: The Archaeology of an Egyptian Frontier Town
Location: On display (Room 69 - Greek and Roman Life, Display Case 25)
Acq notes (exc): Excavated 1884-1885.

Description: Slender jar of Nile silt pottery with flat base, slight belly in lower part and out-curving rim, coated with red slip and burnished on the exterior and inside the rim. Curators Comments:
Similar to White cross-lined ware C75E in decoration, C76H in shape in Petrie’s Corpus of Prehistoric Pottery (1921). 
The design may be a reflection of basketry or might evoke vegetation. The fine lines of the paint work suggest the vessel might originate from Naqada. See discussion in E. Finkenstaedt, Location of Styles of Painting: White Cross lined ware at Naqada. JARCE 18 (1981). 7-10.
Published: A. Scharff, Some Prehistoric Vases in the British Museum and Remarks on Egyptian Prehistory, JEA 14 (1928), p. 261, pl. xxiv.1; 
A.J. Spencer, Early Egypt, (London 1993), fig. 14.

Description: Vase of andesite porphyry: in high-shouldered spheroidal form but without the handles. It is possible, however, that this vessel originally had tubular handles which have since been removed, in which case it would really belong to the class of flat-based spheroidal vases with handles. The mouth is surrounded by a flat-topped rim with a sharp outer edge. The interior is moderately well hollowed.;
Curators Comments: The shoulder of the vase is marked with a rounded groove, of the type made by the drilling of tubular handles. The surface around this marking has been smoothed and polished to blend with the remainder of the vessel. This may indicate that the handles have been cut away for some reason, in which case the vase would be more likely to belong to the First or Second Dynasty.
Bib references: Spencer 1980 / Early Dynastic Objects
Location: On display (Room 64 - Early Egypt, Display Case 12)

Description: A vase of thin copper: with a flat base and convex sides, which turn inwards at the top The spout is rounded and set at a lower angle into the body of the vessel. Traces of linen adhere to the corroded exterior surfaces. One side of the vase has been split by a combination of pressure and corrosion. On the shoulder of the vessel are traces of a hieroglyphic inscription which has been partly erased.;
Dimensions: 7.70 centimetres base ;  10.20 - 11.00 centimetres rim ;  11.40 centimetres;  4.10 centimetres spout ;  2.10 centimetres spout ;
Curators Comments:
The analysis of the metal shows that the vase was made of a fine bronze alloy, certainly the earliest example of the use of bronze in Egypt known so far. The spout, however, consists of arsenical copper.
Bibliography:
W. M. F. Petrie, 'Royal Tombs of the Earliest Dynasties' II, pl. IX, 13.
All the known vases of this type with the double spout belong to the Late Second or Early Third Dynasty. See W. M. F. Petrie, 'Royal Tombs of the Earliest Dynasties' II, pl.IX. 13-14; W. M. F. Description: Spheroidal vase of variegated limestone: with pink, white and black veining. The rim is flat-topped with a sharp outer edge and the interior is fairly well hollowed. On the shoulder of the vase are two tubular handles, both covered by rectangular gold plates with an openwork cross cut in the middle. The handles still contain loops of copper wire, covered with gold foil. The base was drilled right through for ease of hollowing, and would have been plugged by a separate piece of stone, now missing.;
Dimensions: 2.60 centimetres base ;  4.60 centimetres rim ;  6.30 centimetres;  4.50 centimetres;
Curators Comments: The gold detail, and the colour and size of the vessel, suggest that it may well be identical with the vase described in the 'Macgregor Sale Catalogue' (1922), 145, no. 1094, second example. If this identification is correct then the provenance of the vase is known to be Abydos.

Description: Large marl pottery vase with three pierced triangular lug handles on the shoulder and a direct lip rim; decorated in red paint with three scenes of many-oared boats, each with different standards, above which stand human figures and antelopes. Rows of solid triangles, representing mountains are painted between the boats.;
Curators Comments:
Decorated ware D45 series in Petrie’s Corpus of Prehistoric Pottery (1921). A band composed of six wavy lines divided into alternating long and short segments encircles the shoulder below the rim. Below, arranged between the handles (which are outlined with three painted strokes) are three boats with many oars, a double branch frond at the prow, and two central cabins to which a standard has been attached, each bearing a different emblem: a Z-motif or zigzag; a curved motif often identified as double horns; and crossed lines, identified with the crossed arrows, later known as the emblem of the goddess Neith. Above the boats stand human figures. 1) A single man facing right, holding a long curved staff. To the right, an antelope with twisted horns, identified as an addax, is painted above the stern of the boat. 2) A large female figure with arms raised above her head, with a smaller male figure on the left holding her arm. To the right, another male figure, facing right, holds a curved staff. 3) An addax, facing right, is painted above the cabins, and to the right, above the boat’s stern a male figure facing right holds a long curved staff. Vertical and diagonal lines of s-motifs appear around the figures. Rows of solid triangles, probably representing mountains, appear between the boats. 
Similarities in the way the human figures are painted suggested to Smith that the hand of a single painter can be identified connecting this pot to other elaborately painted vessels in other museum. 
Published: C. Torr, Sur quelques pretendus navires egyptiens, L’anthropologie 9 (1898), fig. 1 
J. Capart, Primitive art in Egypt (London 1905) p.118, fig. 91 CT1 (line drawing)
G. Graff, Les peintures sur vases de Naqada I–Naqada II: Nouvelle approche sémiologique de l’iconographie prédynastique, Leuven (2009), cat. no. 287, (double listed as 575 with line drawing considered to portray different pot in error)
A. Smith, Identification d’un potier predynastique. Archeo-Nil 3 (1993),pp. 23-33.
Graff, Les enjeux de l’iconographie des vases pients de Naqada II, Anthropozoologica 46.1, p. 54, 57

Description: Undecorated ostrich egg: intended for use as a container, with an opening in one side.;
Culture: Early Dynastic (Egypt) (Egypt) (Egypt); Nubia and Sudan periods and cultures; A-Group Culture
Curators Comments:
Bibliography:
F. Ll. Griffith, 'Liverpool Annals of Archaeology and Anthropology 8 (Liverpool, 1921), 14.
See C. M. Firth, ‘The Archaeological Survey of Nubia’ (Cairo, 1909-10), pl. II;
N. Strudwick, Masterpieces of Ancient Egypt, London 2006, pp. 30-1.; Strudwick N 2006
'Nubia' designates an area of southern Egypt and northern Sudan. The southern boundary of the area changed over time, but in the pharaonic period this usually lay between the Fourth and Fifth Cataracts of the Nile. The term 'A-Group' refers to an indigenous Nubian culture roughly contemporary with the Egyptian Predynastic cultures Naqada I-III to the First Dynasty (c. 4000-3000 BC). Most of the remains come from cemeteries, from just north of Aswan to south of the Second Nile Cataract. A non-literate culture, it had a strongly Nubian identity, and perhaps numbered 10,000 persons at most. It probably played an important role as an intermediary between the developing Egyptian cultures and the peoples controlling various sources of raw materials to the south. It maintained close contacts with its northern neighbours, and significant amounts of Egyptian ceramics are found in all its sub-phases, particularly in that contemporary with the First Dynasty. At the very end of the Egyptian Predynastic Period, the culture went into decline, probably largely as a result of Egyptian raids that destroyed contemporary political structures. Inscriptions have been found in the region of the Second Cataract which might belong to late Predynastic Egyptian kings.
Faras lies about 40 km to the north of the Second Cataract, and was first excavated by an expedition from Oxford in 1910-12. As well as these very early finds, the site has produced material from all periods, and is particularly renowned for the wall-paintings in its church, dating from the Christian period. The site was most recently examined during the Nubian rescue campaign of the 1960s, by a mission from Poland.
This object was found in grave 31 in the A-Group cemetery; this tomb contained a range of pots and other items, almost all of which are in the British Museum. The age and sex of the owner are not known.
Ostrich eggs are common finds in A-Group burials and in contemporary Egyptian graves. These large items were evidently valuable, and could be used, after consumption of the contents, as quite sturdy drinking or liquid storage vessels. They may perhaps also have possessed some symbolic value, as they are not so common in later periods.
Bib references: Spencer 1980 / Early Dynastic Objects; Strudwick 2006 / Masterpieces of Ancient Egypt
Location: On display (Room 65 - Sudan, Egypt and Nubia, Dispaly Case 3)

Description: Hand-modelled unfired clay figurine of a vulture standing upon a low base. The clay has been tempered with fine organic matter, and the maker has detailed the wings, legs and distinctive neck and head of the vulture.;
Curators Comments: This figure is part of a class of objects which includes representations of cobras, rams, crocodiles, birds etc. They have been found in temple contexts (such as Abydos - see O'Connor in Expedition 12 [1969], 28-39), but are also likely to have found used in funerary and domestic spheres. Fired clay figurines, of similar form, scale and subject matter, are a feature of New Kingdom and Third Intermediate Period settlement sites (see Spencer, Kom Firin I [2008]: 64-7).

Fragment of painted mud-plaster in the form of a cavetto cornice, originally part of the architectural decoration of a mudbrick house, perhaps over and around a doorway. 
The cornice itself is formed from vertical bands of blue, green and red, divided by white areas. Immediately beneath is a torus moulding, with red bands indicating the binding. The area beneath features a  flower-petal motif (in blue), abovea band of alternate red and yellow squares. At the base of the preserved fragment, rectangles of red, blue and green are outlined in black ink, part of a larger design.;
Curators Comments:
It is likely that this is the fragment described in Frankfort and Pendlebury, City of Akhenaten II (London, 1933), 5. The authors describe the discovery of fragments around the entrance to house V.37.1, at the top of the flight of steps.
Published:
T.G.H. James. Egyptian Painting (1985). 38 fig. 40.
M. Stead. Egyptian Life (1994). fig. 12.
Location: On display (Room 61 - Egyptian life and death: the tomb-chapel of Nebamun, Display Case 5)

Description: Limestone window-grille, painted red. Though parts are now missing, the original form is clear: two rows of three, narrow, vertical apertures.;
Curators Comments:
Published:
Frankfort & Pendlebury, City of Akhenaten 2: 67
Window-grilles from Amarna vary significantly in shape and presence of decoration, and examples are known in limestone, wood and even reeds. For other examples, see 
Pharaohs of the Sun (exhibition catalogue, Boston, 1999), no.163
Peet and Woolley, City of Akhenaten I (London, 1923): pl.6
Location: On display (Room 61 - Egyptian life and death: the tomb-chapel of Nebamun, Display Case 5)

Description: A tall jar of red pottery: with an external roll-rim. The sides expand from the rim to the shoulder, then converge to a narrow, rounded base. Between the rim and the shoulder there is a raised ridge. The side of the jar bears an incised inscription, consisting of two signs. Good condition.;
Inscription: Inscription type: mark Inscription position: side Inscription note: Incised.;
Curators Comments:
This jar came to the Museum together with a number of seals of Den; see 1897,0112.21, 1897,0112.23 and 1897,0112.25.
For the mark, cf. W. M. F. Petrie, 'Royal Tombs of the Earliest Dynasties' I, pls. XXXIX, 5 LVI, 1094-8.

Description: Pottery amphora (wine-jar) with two handles and a tall cylindrical neck with a modelled rim. The exterior surface bears a cream slip, and two lines of hieratic text on the surface.;
Inscription: Inscription type: inscription Inscription position: shoulder Inscription script: Hieratic;
Curators Comments:
For similar amphorae from Tell el-Amarna, see P. Rose, The Eighteenth Dynasty Pottery Corpus from Amarna (MEES 83, London, 2007): 128-9, 273 [esp. 589]. Rose notes that hieratic labels are not uncommon on these types of jars, with some also having handles stamped with the production estate.
For hieratic inscription cf. Dept. Conservation photo 1930/4 [of 22.08.2001]

Published:
Hope, Pottery of the Egyptian New Kingdom, (1989), p.102.
Location: On display (Room 61 - Egyptian life and death: the tomb-chapel of Nebamun, Display Case 2, Section D)

Description: Tall jar of dull red pottery: with traces of a pale brown wash. The sides expand from the mouth to the shoulder, then converge to a narrow rounded base. Below the rim there is a raised ridge, close to which there are two incised marks. The jar is sealed with a conical clay stopper, which bears the remains of four impressed inscriptions. Only one impression is now reasonably clear, the other three having disappeared except for slight traces.;
Dimensions: 24.00 centimetres base of seal ;  25.40 centimetres max ;  31.00 centimetres seal alone ;  100.00 centimetres;
Inscription: Inscription type: inscription Inscription position: clay stopper Inscription script: hieroglyphic Inscription translation: Serekh of Den and the official title ḫrp ḥrỉ-ỉb. Inscription note: Two impressions of this text on seal.;

Description: Wooden winnowing-fan or scoop, for holding in the left hand. Such tools were used, in pairs with one held in each hand, to separate the cereal from straw and other debris (with sieves being used for finer sorting).;
